name: Andrew Wakefield
description: Andrew Wakefield is a British former surgeon and medical researcher known for his fraudulent 1998 study published in The Lancet linking the measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) vaccine to autism. This paper sparked a global decline in vaccination rates and a resurgence of measles, mumps, and rubella. Wakefield's theory posited that the MMR vaccine caused intestinal inflammation, leading to the translocation of peptides into the bloodstream and subsequently damaging the brain, resulting in autism. His research was based on a small sample of 12 children, most of whom were recruited through anti-MMR advocacy groups. Subsequent investigations revealed serious ethical violations, undisclosed financial conflicts of interest (linked to planned legal action against vaccine manufacturers), and manipulated data within Wakefield's research. The Lancet retracted the paper in 2010. Wakefield was stripped of his medical license by the General Medical Council in the UK for dishonesty and unethical behavior. Numerous large-scale, well-controlled epidemiological studies conducted worldwide have since found no evidence of a causal link between the MMR vaccine and autism. The scientific consensus is overwhelmingly against Wakefield's claims, and his work is considered a major example of scientific misconduct. Despite the overwhelming evidence against his claims, Wakefield remains a prominent figure in the anti-vaccination movement.
subtitle: Discredited MMR-Autism Link Proponent

claims.evidence: Wakefield's 1998 Lancet paper, anecdotal reports of parents.
claims.criticism: Numerous large-scale epidemiological studies have found no causal link. The Lancet retracted the paper due to fraud and ethical violations.

claims.claim: MMR vaccine causes intestinal inflammation leading to autism.
claims.evidence: Histopathological findings in the 12 children studied in the Lancet paper.
claims.criticism: These findings were inconsistent, and the methodology was flawed. Subsequent investigations revealed manipulated data and biased patient selection.

claims.claim: Single measles vaccine is safer than the combined MMR vaccine.
claims.evidence: Wakefield's promotion of single vaccines after his MMR claims.
claims.criticism: Single measles vaccines require multiple injections, increasing the risk of infection and reducing overall vaccination rates. They are not widely available or recommended.

keyFigures.name: Andrew Wakefield
keyFigures.role: Lead author of the discredited Lancet paper.

keyFigures.name: Brian Deer
keyFigures.role: Journalist who exposed the fraud and ethical violations in Wakefield's research.

keyFigures.name: Fiona Godlee
keyFigures.role: Editor-in-chief of the BMJ who published articles detailing Wakefield's misconduct.

mythologyConnections: Fear of medical technology as a modern-day Prometheus myth
The archetype of the 'fallen hero' – a trusted figure who betrays public trust.

timeline.event: Wakefield stripped of his medical license.
Wakefield continues to promote anti-vaccination views.
